definitions.definition: A First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure where elements are added at the end and removed from the front.
definitions.example: Example: A line of people waiting for a movie ticket can be represented as a queue.

definitions.definition: The process of adding an element to the end of the queue.
definitions.example: Example: Adding a person to the end of the line.

definitions.definition: The process of removing an element from the front of the queue.
definitions.example: Example: Removing a person from the front of the line.

definitions.definition: The front of the queue is the position where the oldest element is stored.
definitions.example: Example: The front of a queue is where the first person in the line is waiting.

definitions.definition: The rear of the queue is the position where the newest element is stored.
definitions.example: Example: The rear of a queue is where a new person joins the line.

definitions.definition: The basic operations that can be performed on a queue are enqueue, dequeue, isEmpty, isFull, peek, and size.
definitions.example: Example: These operations are essential for managing a queue.

definitions.definition: A data structure that follows the FIFO principle, where elements are added and removed based on their insertion order.
definitions.example: Example: A queue can be used to manage a print queue or a job scheduler.

definitions.definition: A principle that states the first element added to the queue will be the first one to be removed.
definitions.example: Example: A printer's print queue follows the FIFO principle.

definitions.definition: A principle that states the last element added to the queue will be the first one to be removed.
definitions.example: Example: A stack follows the LIFO principle.

definitions.definition: A queue that has no elements.
definitions.example: Example: A queue with no people waiting in line is an empty queue.

definitions.definition: A queue that has reached its maximum capacity.
definitions.example: Example: A full queue is when there is no more space to add new elements.

definitions.definition: The number of elements currently stored in the queue.
definitions.example: Example: The size of a queue can be used to determine if it is full or empty.

definitions.definition: A non-destructive operation that allows you to access the element at the front of the queue without removing it.
definitions.example: Example: A peek operation can be used to check the next person in line without removing them.

definitions.definition: A type of queue where the front and rear indices wrap around when they reach the end of the array.
definitions.example: Example: A circular queue can be used to efficiently manage a queue with a fixed size.

definitions.definition: A type of queue where each element is a separate object and is linked to the next element in the queue.
definitions.example: Example: A linked queue can be used to efficiently manage a large queue.

definitions.definition: A type of queue where elements are stored in an array and the front and rear indices are used to manage the queue.
definitions.example: Example: An array queue can be used to efficiently manage a queue with a fixed size.

definitions.definition: The process of designing and implementing a queue data structure using an array, linked list, or other data structures.
definitions.example: Example: A queue implementation can be used to manage a print queue or a job scheduler.
